title: CNNs & Computer Vision Quiz
description: Test your knowledge on Convolution, Filters, Max Pooling, and Transfer Learning.

questions.question: What is the primary reason standard, Fully-Connected Artificial Neural Networks are terrible for processing large, high-resolution images?

options.text: Every pixel connecting to every neuron creates millions of useless weights, destroying training times and ignoring fundamental spatial geometry (like pixels next to each other forming a shape).
They cannot process RGB color values, only black and white floating points.
They only compile successfully on macOS environments.
Python's `numpy` library lacks the precision required to multiply pixel values.

questions.question: In a CNN, what is a 'Filter' (or Kernel)?

options.text: A physical Instagram-style aesthetic filter applied before processing.
A tiny grid of weights (e.g., 3x3) that slides continuously across the image mathematically detecting specific local features like horizontal edges, corners, or textures.
An automated system that removes null values from the image data.
A spam detection tool for text.

questions.question: What is the core mathematical function of a 'Max Pooling' layer located deep within a CNN architecture?

options.text: It violently adds random noise back into the clean image to prevent overfitting.
It artificially increases the resolution of a pixelated image to 4K.
It drastically shrinks and downsamples the spatial dimensions of the feature map to reduce computation load while simultaneously retaining the most prominently activated core features.
It flattens the image into a 1-dimensional array immediately upon boot.

questions.question: How does a CNN ultimately make a final classification (like predicting 'Dog' vs 'Cat') after sliding filters over an image?

options.text: It uses an SQL JOIN query.

It finally 'Flattens' the 2D feature maps from the deep layers into a simple 1D array and feeds that array directly into a perfectly standard Fully-Connected network classifier located at the very end.
It throws away the data and queries a database instead.
It outputs raw binary coordinates to the user.

questions.question: What technique allows a junior developer with a single laptop to build a world-class tumor-detection AI model without training it mathematically from scratch on millions of normal images?

options.text: Unsupervised Dimensionality Reduction.

Downloading a massive pre-trained open-source CNN (like ResNet) that already understands elementary shapes and features, attaching a completely custom final classification layer to it, and retraining purely that final layer. (Transfer Learning).
Writing the entire algorithm entirely in manual C++ code.
Utilizing k-Nearest Neighbors.
